Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd tell myself I was going to study for like 32 hours a week, and sometimes I did sit at my desk for that long, but a lot of it wasn't productive because my anxiety would just take over. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to make diagrams and color-coded notes, but without reliable internet at home, I couldn't always access what I needed when I needed it. I mostly crammed things in whenever I could get to campus or a library with wifi.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely not regular at all. I'd have these bursts of intense studying right before deadlines, then I'd crash and avoid everything for days because I was so exhausted and stressed. Some weeks I barely touched anything, other weeks I was up until 3am trying to catch up. It really depended on what was due and honestly, how bad my anxiety was on any given day.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered something was due or when I got an email notification that made my heart drop. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, which happened a lot because my attendance was so low. The anxiety of not knowing what I was missing actually made me avoid logging in sometimes, which I know sounds backwards.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS. Every time I logged in it felt like facing a mountain of things I hadn't done, which just triggered more anxiety. The closest I got to routine was when some of my peers from the peer support group would remind me to check certain things, and we'd kind of do it together virtually. That helped a bit, but it never became automatic for me.
Q5: The video lectures were the most helpful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them multiple times to understand concepts. I watched over four hours worth, which might not sound like much but for me it was significant. I also appreciated when instructors used diagrams or visual demonstrations. The discussion boards were okay when other students explained things in different ways than the professor did.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs because they felt overwhelming and I couldn't process dense text when I was anxious. I also barely participated in discussions - only posted like six times all semester. The optional practice quizzes I kept putting off because I was afraid of seeing how much I didn't know. It's like I was protecting myself from bad news, even though that made everything worse.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or when it directly connected to an assignment that was due soon. If I couldn't see how something would help me pass, my brain just couldn't prioritize it. Visual content that I could actually understand without rereading five times felt worthwhile. When my peer support group said something was important, that also made me more likely to do it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're drowning and barely completing half your assignments, optional stuff feels impossible to justify. Required activities at least had deadlines that forced me to act, even if it was last minute. Optional things just sat there making me feel guilty that I wasn't doing them, adding to my stress without any structure to push me through.
Q9: Yes, definitely. Once I downloaded materials, I'd study them offline a lot because I don't have reliable internet at home. I'd print things out at the library and annotate them by hand, make visual study guides on paper, watch downloaded videos on my laptop without wifi. I also used some external YouTube videos to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I was studying from printed notes or my handwritten visual summaries for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd be at home without internet, going over materials I'd saved, trying to understand them. Or I'd be in study sessions with peers from the support group, working through problems together in person. The LMS only saw me when I had internet access, which wasn't always.
Q11: All the time, yes. I'd download a video lecture once, then watch it three or four more times offline trying to understand it. Same with PDF slides - I'd access them once to download, then study from the downloaded version for days. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my login frequency would suggest, at least for the things I did manage to study.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. My anxiety was through the roof, I was sleeping terribly, and I just couldn't focus on anything. I stopped attending classes almost entirely - my attendance dropped to basically nothing. The workload from all eleven of my online courses felt impossible, and I started avoiding the LMS because logging in just reminded me how far behind I was. It became this vicious cycle.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those things affected me constantly. The workload from taking eleven courses was crushing, and I was always confused about what was due when. Fatigue from anxiety-induced insomnia made it hard to concentrate even when I did try to study. I'd log into the LMS intending to work, then just stare at the screen feeling overwhelmed and end up closing it without doing anything meaningful.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be racing with anxious thoughts about everything I hadn't done. I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open an assignment, look at it for twenty minutes feeling paralyzed, then close it without writing anything. The LMS would show I accessed it, but I wasn't really there mentally.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they were important - especially when I knew they were important, actually. The more important something was, the more anxiety it triggered, and the more I avoided it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel worse because now I had even less time. It's like my brain was trying to protect me from failure by not trying, which guaranteed failure.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me to act, but usually way too late. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then panic-submit something half-finished. My average response time was over eight hours after I'd access something, which shows how much I'd procrastinate even after logging in. Deadlines created these intense stress spikes that weren't healthy but were the only thing that overcame my avoidance.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it helped a little and made me feel like maybe I could do this. But negative feedback or low grades would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for days. I needed feedback to improve, but my anxiety made it really hard to face. Sometimes I wouldn't even look at my grades because I couldn't handle seeing them.
Q18: The course structure honestly made things harder for me. Having so many different components and deadlines to track across eleven courses was overwhelming. I needed more scaffolding and reminders, not just content dumps. The peer support intervention actually helped because it gave me some human structure and accountability, which the course itself didn't provide enough of for someone struggling like me.
Q19: The lack of clear visual roadmaps or progress tracking made engagement harder - I'm a visual learner and I needed to see where I was and where I needed to go. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually hurt me because I need external structure. What made it easier was when content was broken into smaller chunks and when there were visual elements. The peer support connection was the most helpful design element, honestly.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, browse around, but then not submit work because I was too anxious about it not being good enough. Or I'd start assignments but not finish them because perfectionism paralyzed me. Sometimes high activity is actually just anxious checking and rechecking without productive output. The fear of failure can make you engage with materials without ever committing to submitting anything.
Q21: That wasn't my experience at all - I had low activity AND low performance. But I can imagine students who are efficient, who download everything once and study effectively offline, or who already know the material and just need to submit work. Some people don't need to engage much with the LMS to learn. For me though, low engagement reflected genuine struggle, not efficiency.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, which gives some picture of engagement patterns. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement - whether you were actually focused or just staring blankly. They miss offline studying, emotional state, life circumstances, anxiety levels. They can't tell if you watched a video three times because you were struggling or because you were thorough. They miss so much context.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through motions. It means being mentally present, asking questions, wanting to understand rather than just pass. For me, true engagement happened in rare moments when my anxiety quieted enough to actually think. It's about investment and attention, which are internal things that don't always show up in clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite my anxiety, or how much I studied offline without internet. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that kept me from giving up entirely, or the tears of frustration when I couldn't focus. It wouldn't show that my mood actually improved by the end because of the support I received, even though my grades didn't. It wouldn't show that I'm not lazy - I'm struggling with real mental health challenges while trying to learn.
